title: Coppice willow withy ties
subtitle: Flexible willow ties for the kitchen garden and allotment
excerpt: Willow withies; thin coppiced rods split lengthways and twisted while green; are the traditional garden tie. They hold climbing beans, sweet peas, and tomatoes to their supports without wire or plastic and decompose usefully at the end of the season. A beginner green-woodwork project from a small bundle of freshly cut willow.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for kitchen-garden ties; willow withy-work for garden use documented in Victorian and Edwardian kitchen-garden manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently cut timber, still high in moisture content. Willow withies must be used green; dry willow rods snap rather than flex.
A thin flexible stem or split stem of willow, used for tying or weaving. In the kitchen garden, a withy is the natural equivalent of a plant tie.

content.text: willow rod, twisted and looped to hold a plant stem to a cane or stake. The twist in the withy keeps the loop taut without tightening as the plant grows; once a plant tie is no longer needed, the withy can be composted. The project needs only secateurs and a bundle of fresh willow; it is the lowest-barrier introduction to coppice craft.

Cut first-year or second-year coppice willow rods in early spring before the buds break, or in autumn after leaf fall. Osier willow (Salix viminalis) and common sallow (Salix cinerea) are both reliable. The rods should be 3 to 6 mm in diameter at the base, straight and free of side shoots for at least 50 cm. A bundle of 20 rods gives 60 to 80 withies depending on diameter.
Cut the rods to length with secateurs: 40 to 60 cm is the useful range. Longer withies tie larger plants; shorter ones work for small seedlings and pot plants.

Split each rod in half lengthways: hold the thick end in one hand, push the thumbnail of the other hand into the pith at the tip end, and pull the two halves apart. The split follows the grain naturally; hold both halves and steer the split to keep it central by bending the thicker half away from the thinner.

To make a withy tie: take a split half-rod. Hold the thick end between thumb and forefinger. Roll the rod between the fingers so the flat (pith) face turns outward as the rod twists. After three or four full turns, the rod holds the twist without further hand pressure. Loop the twisted withy around the plant stem and stake; the flat face on the outside of the loop protects the plant stem from bruising.
Tuck the tip of the withy under the loop on the stake side to secure it. No knot is needed; the twisted withy grips itself.

Freshly cut unsplit rods keep for 3 to 4 days in a cool shaded place. Once split, use within a day; split withies dry and become brittle more quickly than whole rods. If you need withies later in the season, soak dry rods in water for 24 hours before splitting; they will not recover full green flexibility but will be workable.
Hazel withies: hazel rods split to withies in the same way and are slightly stiffer; useful for tying heavier stems.
Unsplit rod as a tie: a thin (3 mm) whole rod, twisted on itself, makes a stronger tie than a split half-rod; suitable for young fruit trees tied to a stake.
At the end of the season

Remove willow withies before they set permanently around the plant stem. They will have dried rigid by midsummer; cut rather than unwind, and add to the compost heap. Willow decomposes quickly and adds no harmful residues.

items.symptom: Rod snaps rather than splitting when pulled apart
items.cause: Rod is too dry; or the split was started at the thick end rather than the tip
items.fix: Always start the split from the tip end. If the rod snaps, soak the bundle for 30 minutes in water and try again. Dry rods will not split cleanly even after soaking; use them for staking rather than tieing.

items.symptom: Withy untwists and comes loose in the first week
items.cause: Twist not tight enough before looping around the plant; or rod too dry when applied
items.fix: Add two more turns before looping. Work with rods cut within the previous 12 hours. Withies that untwist on dry rods can be replaced with a fresh green one the following morning.
